---
title: "Southern Water Violet (Viola vittata)"
slug: "viola-vittata"
group: "Plantae"
image: "https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/626923081/medium.jpg"        # keep for thumbnails
image-alt: "Southern Water Violet"  # keep for a11y
tax_kingdom: "Plantae"
tax_class: "Magnoliopsida"
tax_order: "Malpighiales"
tax_family: "Violaceae"
categories: ["Plantae", "Magnoliopsida", "Malpighiales", "Violaceae"]   # these drive the chips + filtering
freeze: true
---

<figure class="species-figure">

<img src="https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/626923081/medium.jpg" alt="Southern Water Violet" class="species-photo"/>

<figcaption class="photo-credit">

Photo: <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/people/kellyobear">
kellyobear</a> (CC BY-NC) via <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/344098443">iNaturalist</a>

</figcaption>

</figure>

## Taxonomy

-   **Class:** Magnoliopsida
-   **Order:** Malpighiales
-   **Family:** Violaceae

## Occurrence at DeLuca Preserve

-   [DeLuca Bio Blitz Species Observations](https://www.inaturalist.org/observations?project_id=191727&subview=map&taxon_id=1392343)

## Conservation & rarity context

-   IUCN: Not Listed
-   **Rarity note:** Common species in Florida found in moist to wet habitats.

## Natural history notes

A small wildflower characterized by its narrow, grass-like leaves and delicate white to pale lavender flowers with purple markings. In Florida, it is found in moist to wet habitats such as marsh edges, wet flatwoods, and seepage areas. It typically blooms during the cooler to warmer months and often occurs as a subtle component of wetland groundcover vegetation.

## References / links

-   [iNaturalist species page](https://www.inaturalist.org/taxa/1392343-Viola-vittata)
-   [Local profile](https://fsus.ncbg.unc.edu/cust/2025/main.php?pg=show-taxon-detail.php&taxonid=3611)
